coredata.dc:title: C8-structured carbon quantum dots: Synthesis, blue and green double luminescence, and origins of surface defects
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xifang
Zhang, Wenxia
Wang, Qianjin
Fan, Jiyang
coredata.dc:description: Nanoscale carbon generally exists as nanodiamond or graphite quantum dot (QD). In this study, we report the first synthesis of the third allotrope carbon QDs with an average size of 2nm through carbonization of sucrose and study their photoluminescence properties. These QDs have a body-centered cubic structure and each lattice point is composed of eight atoms which form a sub-cube (so called C8 crystal structure). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction confirm the C8 structure of the synthesized nanocrystallites. The C8 QDs exhibit double-band luminescence with two peaks centered at around 432 and 520nm. The study based on the photoluminescence, UV–Vis absorption, Fourier-transform infrared, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies reveals that the green emission originates from the CO related surface defect whereas the blue emission originates from another surface defect.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (c) TEM images of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs). (b) And the inset of (c) show the high-resolution images of some particles. Several lattice spacings are shown. (d) Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CQDs. (e) Size distribution of the CQDs. (f) Configuration of the C8 crystal structure. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

X-ray diffraction patterns of two as-prepared CQDs powders. The red vertical lines denote the standard diffraction peak positions of four groups of lattice planes of the C8 carbon film [48]. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) and (b) PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the as-prepared CQDs aqueous solution. (c) PL and (d) PLE spectra of the CQDs aqueous solution after addition of NaOH followed by UV-light irradiation for 9h. (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra. (f) Time-resolved PL spectra of the as-prepared and treated (for 9h) CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fourier-transform infrared spectra of the as-prepared and treated (for 9h) CQDs. (b) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of the CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectrum of (c) C 1s and (d) O 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Electron energy loss spectra of C(1s) core electron excitation in as-prepared CQDs. The green vertical lines denote the peaks. (b) Raman spectra of the as-prepared and treated (for 9h) CQDs. Three different signal zones are marked. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Indexing of the electron diffraction pattern of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: d exp Refers to the measured value for the CQDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: d lit Refers to the value for the C8 carbon films from the literature [48].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Lifetime (τ) and relative amplitude (R) of the three components of the fit of the time-resolved PL spectrum of the as-prepared and treated (for 9h) colloidal CQDs.

head.ce:title: C8-structured carbon quantum dots: Synthesis, blue and green double luminescence, and origins of surface defects

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures and Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures and Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoscale carbon generally exists as nanodiamond or graphite quantum dot (QD). In this study, we report the first synthesis of the third allotrope carbon QDs with an average size of 2nm through carbonization of sucrose and study their photoluminescence properties. These QDs have a body-centered cubic structure and each lattice point is composed of eight atoms which form a sub-cube (so called C8 crystal structure). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction confirm the C8 structure of the synthesized nanocrystallites. The C8 QDs exhibit double-band luminescence with two peaks centered at around 432 and 520nm. The study based on the photoluminescence, UV–Vis absorption, Fourier-transform infrared, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies reveals that the green emission originates from the CO related surface defect whereas the blue emission originates from another surface defect.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoparticles, including nanodiamonds (NDs) and graphite quantum dots (QDs), have attracted great interest in the past few years [1–4] due to their innate biocompatibility, material abundance, superior thermal conductivity [5], and robust near-infrared to near-UV luminescence. The graphite QD a few atomic layers in thickness is also called graphene QD [6]. Compared with the cases of other group IV nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes [7] and Si QDs [8], people know less about the carbon nanoparticles. Most NDs were prepared through explosive detonation [9]. Their luminescence originates from the interior defects, such as the red luminescent nitrogen-vacancy defects created by high-energy irradiation [10]. They are favorable materials for biological imaging and drug delivery [11–13]. The graphite QDs were synthesized by graphitization of NDs [14,15] and by various chemical methods [16–22]. They generally show luminescence in the green to violet spectral region, occasionally in the near-infrared region [23]. They are suited for biological imaging [19,21] and delivery of molecules into cells [24]. Graphene QDs are good candidates for qubit sources and integrated charge sensors applied in quantum computation [25,26]. They have shown promise in applications of light emitting diodes, lasers, and solar cells [27–31]. Except for the intentionally created nitrogen-vacancy defects in the NDs, the origins of most of the observed light emissions in the carbon nanoparticles are still under debate. The NDs have larger energy gaps than that of bulk diamond (5.5eV) due to the quantum confinement effect [32]. The graphene QDs possibly have energy gaps smaller than 1eV [33] and in these QDs both fluorescence and phosphorescence can take place [34]. Surface shape [35], modified chemical composition [36–38], and applied electric or magnetic field [39,40] all can significantly influence the electronic structures of the graphene QDs. Little has been known about the electronic structure of the graphite QDs. The photon reabsorption that usually occurs in colloids makes the analysis of the luminescence properties of the carbon quantum dots even difficult [41].

Actually, carbon can exist in a third crystal form: C8 structure. The C8-structured carbon material was first discovered in the plasma deposited carbon film [42,43]. It has a unique body-centered cubic structure, in which each lattice point comprises eight carbon atoms which form a sub-cube; each carbon atom in the C8 crystal and its four nearest neighbors form a distorted tetrahedron (Fig. 1 f). C8 carbon has a mass density of 4.1g/cm3 which is larger than that of cubic diamond by 15% [43]. The C8-like carbon cubes with sizes of over 50nm had been discovered in the laser ablated carbon films and graphites [44–46]. A two-dimensional analogue of C8 with properties similar to that of graphene had been theoretically predicted [47]. But to date, there has been no report on the C8-structured carbon quantum dot (CQD). Herein, we report the first synthesis of the C8-structured CQDs and study their special double-band luminescence. Our study reveals that the luminescence in the CQDs originates from the surface defects.

20g sucrose and 40mL oil acid were mixed in an oil bath and heated at 215°C for 5min in argon atmosphere under magnetic stirring. Afterwards, the reacted solution was cooled down to room temperature. The loose solid network of the intermediate product in the solution was removed by centrifugation. The resulting solution was yellow. The deionized water was then added into this solution to serve as the solvent for the synthesized hydrophilic carbon nanoparticles. The residual oil acid in the solution was eliminated by addition and subsequent separation of hexane a few times (oil acid dissolves readily in hexane). The resultant aqueous solution was uniform and it contained carbon nanoparticles. This solution was divided into three shares (3mL per share). One share served as the as-prepared sample. The second share was diluted nine times with deionized water. 0.15g NaOH was added into the third share, followed by irradiation by UV light (from 50W halogen lamp) for up to 9h.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a Fluorolog 3-TCSPC spectrofluorometer (HORIBA JOBIN YVON) equipped with a xenon lamp and a NanoLED-375LH (pulse duration: 750ps). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U-3900 UV/Vis spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted using a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEM-200CX TEM operating at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a PHI 5000 VersaProbe spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα source. X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) was performed on a Smartlab (3) X-ray diffractometer. Electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) was performed on a FEI TECNAIF20S-TW2N TEM with a Gatan GIF 994 system by taking the silicon nitride film (thickness: 15nm) as the nanoparticle supporting film. Raman spectra were obtained using a Bruker FT-Raman (MulitRAM) Raman spectrometer equipped with an air-cooled Nd:YAG laser to deliver an excitation wavelength of 1064nm.

Fig. 1a and c show the TEM images of the synthesized nanoparticles. The high-resolution images of a few nanoparticles are given in Fig. 1b and the inset of Fig. 1c. The lattice fringes of the particles can clearly be seen, indicating that they are single crystals. The lattice spacing is measured to be around 3.02 Å, corresponding to that of the (110) planes of C8 carbon [43,48]. The nanoparticles have a narrow size distribution, as shown in Fig. 1(e). This size distribution was obtained by measuring one by one the sizes of the 224 particles that are displayed in the TEM images. The most probable diameter is about 2nm. The selected area electron diffraction pattern further confirms the C8 structure of the nanoparticles (Fig. 1d). The lattice spacings acquired from the diffraction pattern are listed in Table 1 . These values are consistent with those of the C8 carbon films within the experimental error. Note that the blurry clouds surrounding the sharp rings in the pattern suggest that there are also some amorphous nanoparticles in the sample. The diameters of the blurred rings are hard to measure accurately and this explains the small difference between our measured data and that of the C8 carbon film. The C8 CQDs are simply called CQDs in the following sections.

The X-ray diffraction measurement further confirms the C8 crystal structure of the synthesized carbon quantum dots. Fig. 2 shows the XRD spectra of two as-prepared CQDs powders. Four diffraction peaks are identified, which show good consistence with those of the C8 carbon film in peak position [48]. But the peaks are wide, indicating that the nanoparticles are very small. The average diameter of the CQDs can be calculated by using the Scherrer equation
D = 0.89 λ / ( β cos
θ )
, where D is the average diameter of the CQDs, λ =0.154056nm is the wavelength of the X ray, β is the full width at half maximum of the diffraction peak, θ is the corresponding Bragg diffraction angle. The calculation concerning the 100 peak gives rise to an average diameter of 1.2nm, which is a little smaller than that obtained from the TEM observation (1.9nm) and this difference may arise from the inaccuracy of the β value caused by the overlap of the 100 and 110 peaks.

Fig. 3 a shows the PL spectra of the as-prepared CQDs aqueous solution. The typical spectrum comprises two emission peaks: one blue peak centered at around 432nm and another green peak centered at around 520nm. The relative intensity of the blue emission decreases and that of the green emission increases with increasing excitation wavelength. These contrary variation trends suggest their different origins. When the excitation wavelength exceeds 400nm, the blue emission vanishes gradually. The double-band PL spectrum can be well fit by using a double-Gaussian function (Fig. S1). The fitted components have maxima at around 432 and 520nm [note Fig. S1(a) contains an additional violet component at 390nm]. Their peak positions remain nearly unchanged as the excitation wavelength increases, suggesting their origins of defect states rather than interband transitions, since the latter type of luminescence should exhibit quantum size effect. Note that the luminescence properties of the C8 CQDs are different from that of the C8-like carbon cubes, which have a very wide PL band with a peak wavelength smaller than 400nm [45]. These distinct luminescence properties are caused by their different dimensions and different surface structures. Fig. 3b displays the PLE spectra of the CQDs. Each spectrum comprises three peaks centered at about 325, 367, and 415nm, respectively. From the variation laws of the emission and excitation spectra, we deduce that the 325nm excitation corresponds to the 432nm emission and the 415nm excitation corresponds to the 520nm emission. The 367nm excitation contributes to both blue and green emissions; thereby it should arise from a third type of defect. The 415nm excitation is weak and this is because that the 325nm excitation can also contribute to the green emission through reabsorption of its corresponding 432 emission by the CQDs in the solution [41]. In fact, for a diluted solution in which the light reabsorption is substantially suppressed, the 415nm excitation becomes more pronounced (Fig. S2); meanwhile, the green emission becomes weaker and the blue emission becomes stronger owing to suppressed light reabsorption.

What are the origins of the blue and green emissions? The previous studies on Si nanoparticles had demonstrated that the SiO bonds on their surfaces can result in the transition of the quantum confinement luminescence to the SiO related fixed luminescence [49]. Similarly, for the currently studied CQDs, the luminescence may stem from the CO related fluorophore on the particle surface, including carboxyl (C(O)OH), ester (C(O)O), and carbonyl (CO) groups. To investigate the role of the CO bonds in the luminescence of the CQDs, we study the luminescence properties of the CQDs aqueous solution after addition of NaOH and irradiation by UV light (for 9h). The emission and excitation spectra are shown in Fig. 3c and d. As expected, the green emission vanishes after the treatment, so does its corresponding 415nm excitation. This is because that the combined effect of alkali and UV-light results in the degradation of the CO bonds in the ester groups into other chemical bonds [50].

Fig. 3e shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CQDs. For the as-prepared CQDs, the strong absorption at 282nm is ascribed to the π→π∗ transition of the conjugated CO bond. The 225nm peak is attributed to the π→π∗ transition of the CCCO group. The 325, 367, and 415nm absorptions observed in the PLE spectra are all relatively weak and their superposition gives rise to the broad lower-energy side shoulder of the 282nm peak. Considering the low intensities and peak positions, the absorption at 415nm may arise from the n →π∗ transition or the overtone π→π∗ transition of the C(O)O group, whereas the 325-nm absorption may originate from the n →π∗ transition of the CCCO group. The 367nm peak may arise from the overtone transition of the oxygen bridged bond (ROR′). The absorption at 282nm decreases continuously with time after introduction of alkali and irradiation by UV light and the 225nm absorption decreases too. These phenomena indicate that the alkali+UV-light treatment can indeed cause degradation of the CO-related chemical groups. As other absorption bands decrease, an absorption peak at 259nm becomes obvious. This absorption may be assigned to the π→π∗ transition of the conjugated CC bonds.

The decay laws of the blue and green light emissions are distinct. Fig. 3f shows the time-resolved PL spectra of the as-prepared and treated CQDs aqueous solutions. Each decay curve is well fit using a triple-exponential function. The corresponding lifetimes and relative amplitudes of the three components of the fit are summarized in Table 2 . Each decay curve of the as-prepared (treated) CQDs for 440nm (450nm) emission comprises a component with lifetime of 6.0ns (5.6ns) and relative amplitude of 54% (55%). The large proportion implies that this component should be assigned to the blue emission band. There is another slow component of 41.2ns (24.3ns) for the as-prepared (treated) CQDs, which has small relative amplitude of 4% (6%). A third fastest-decay component has a lifetime of 2.7ns (2.4ns) and a relative component of 42% (39%). It corresponds to a narrow ultraviolet emission band which lies on the higher-energy-side shoulder of the blue emission band, as shown in Fig. 3a and c (see also Figs. S1 and S2). For the decay curve measured at 525nm for the as-prepared CQDs, the proportion of the 5.5ns component decreases to 29%, whereas the proportion of the slowest component (86.7ns) increases to 40%, demonstrating that the latter should correspond to the green emission band. The third component with a time constant of 1.6ns is the instrumental response.

Fig. 4 a displays the infrared absorption spectra of the CQDs. For the as-prepared CQDs, the absorption signals at around 1001 and 1023cm−1 are ascribed to the COC stretching vibrations [51]. The band at 1718cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching vibration. The absorption at 1419cm−1 is attributed to the OH in-plane deformation vibration or CH x (x =2, 3) bending vibration. The 795cm−1 peak is ascribed to the deformation vibration of ring hydrogen. The peaks at 2837 and 2939cm−1 are assigned to the CH x (x =2, 3) stretching vibrations. The wide strong absorption bands centered at 1646, 2062, and 3475cm−1 are mainly attributed to the residual water molecules in the KBr pellet. However, the 1646cm−1 peak may also be partially contributed by the CC stretching vibration. These infrared absorption features confirm the existence of the CO and the CC bonds on the CQDs surfaces. The infrared spectrum of the treated CQDs shows similar features, except that the CO absorption at 1718cm−1 diminishes substantially. Such a variation supports the aforementioned speculation of the suppression of the CO bonds by the treatment.

The XPS measurement provides more information on the surface structures of the CQDs. Fig. 4b shows the full-band XPS spectra of the as-prepared and treated CQDs. The C 1s and O 1s signals are strong in both as-prepared and treated CQDs. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s are shown in Fig. 4c. For the as-prepared CQDs, the strong peak at 284.0eV is ascribed to the binding energy of C8 carbon atoms, whose position deviates dramatically from that of carbon atoms in the graphite and diamond.

The CO signals are strong, they are C∗(C(O)O) at 285.5eV [52], C(O)OC∗ at 286.8eV [53], and C∗ O at 287.7eV [54]. The atoms marked with an asterisk represent the atoms to which the binding energy corresponds. The 292.1 and 295.0eV peaks are the C 1s satellite signals [55,56]. The CQDs show a different C 1s spectrum after the treatment with alkali and UV light. The C8 1s signal remains unchanged. In contrast, the C(O)O signals decrease significantly. Meantime, the C∗ O or C∗(O)O signal at 288.4eV [57] becomes obvious, suggesting that partial ester groups [C(O)O] have transformed into the carbonyl groups (RCOR′). Fig. 4d shows the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s. For the as-prepared CQDs, the strong peak at 531.8eV is assigned to C(O∗)O [58]. The weak peak at 533.9eV arises from C(O)O∗ [52]. Another weak peak at 530.2eV is ascribed to Rh (impurity)-related oxygen [59]. After the treatment, the C(O∗)O signal at 531.5eV decreases highly. In contrast, the Rh-related oxygen signal at 530.3eV becomes much stronger. Such a change suggests that the 530.3eV signal may arise from the oxygen bridged bond (ROR′), and the treatment with alkali and UV light may have turned a large proportion of the C(O)O groups into ROR′. It should be noted that the peak at 534.7eV in the treated CQDs is ascribed to the water molecules absorbed by the NaOH powder mixed in the CQDs.

The theoretical study of bulk C8 carbon has indicated that it is an insulator with a direct bandgap of 6.5–11eV and an indirect bandgap of 5.5eV [60]. This indirect bandgap is close to the bandgap of diamond, this character combined with the fact that both C8 carbon and diamond have cubic crystal structures indicates that C8 behaves more like diamond than graphite in properties. In general, the bandgap of the C8 CQDs should be wider than that of bulk material due to the quantum confinement effect. Therefore, the observed blue and green emission bands in the C8 CQDs cannot originate from the interband transition. The foregoing characterizations have revealed that the green emission band of the CQDs vanishes after the treatment of alkali and UV light. On the other hand, the combined analysis of the UV–Vis absorption, infrared absorption, and XPS measurements suggests that most of the ester groups on the CQD surface degrade after the treatment. Combining such information together, we conclude that the green emission is related to the ester groups on the CQD surface. The current information is insufficient for definitely identifying the specific surface defect that results in the blue emission; but we do know one of its characters, this surface defect does not vanish after the alkali and UV-light treatment.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the EELS spectra of the C(1s) core electron excitation in the as-prepared CQDs. The comparison shows that the C8 EELS spectra are quite different from that of diamond or graphite [61,62]. The 283eV peak results from the 1s to π∗ transition, and its relative intensity is much weaker than that of graphite, but stronger than that of diamond. The arising of this peak reveals the presence of sp2 carbon atoms on the surface of the C8 CQD, which is very similar to the case of the diamond. The strongest and dominant band in the EELS spectrum rises at 291eV, reaches a maximum at around 297eV, and then falls slowly on the higher energy side. This strong band is attributed to the 1s to conduction band excitation, and its shape reflects the shape of the density of states curve of the conduction band of C8 CQDs weighed by the transition probability. There are two additional weak and wide bands on the higher energy side of the spectrum with two maxima at around 323 and 363eV. These two peaks and the 283eV peak (1s to π∗ excitation) are uniformly spaced by 40eV, which is close to the plasmon energy of carbon (
E
p
= ℏ
ω
p
,
ω
p
is the plasmon frequency), suggesting that the 323 and 363eV peaks arise from the combined 1s to π∗ and plasmon excitation. However, it should be noted that the long tail in the EELS spectrum extending from 310 to 390eV contains also the contribution of the electron transition from 1s to the band above the conduction band.

The Raman spectra of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 5(b). For the as-prepared CQDs, the Raman signals are divided into three regions. The region between 1130 and 2265cm−1 (Zone II) and the region between 2265 and 3400cm−1 (Zone III) are two dominant regions, and the usual Raman signals of various types of carbon materials are subjected to arise in these regions [3,63]; for example, the Zone II can accommodate the diamond signal at 1328cm−1, the D, G, and D′ bands of graphene/graphite, and some signals from surface chemical bonds; whereas the Zone III can accommodate the G′, D+D′, and 2G bands of graphene/graphite. The Raman spectra of the diamond and graphene usually consist of a couple of sharp lines. In contrast, Fig. 5(b) shows that the two Raman bands of C8 CQDs are very wide, due to the widening of the spectrum by the phonon confinement effect and due to the additional contribution from the surface chemical bonds including OH and CC at around 1640cm−1 as well as CO at 1710–1760cm−1. There is a third band centered at around 366cm−1 (Zone I) in the Raman spectrum of the as-prepared CQDs and it vanishes in the spectrum of the treated CQDs, suggesting that it may be the fluorescence signal. By contrast, the Raman bands in the Zones II and III are maintained for the treated CQDs. Note the sharp lines in the Raman spectrum of the treated CQDs are attributed to chemical bonds created by the NaOH treatment.

Usually, the chemical synthesis of carbon materials yields graphite nanoparticles, then why can the current chemical synthesis give rise to the CQDs with very special C8 crystal structure? The aqueous solvent has been widely used as the reaction solvent for the synthesis of carbon nanoparticles; in this condition and under ambient pressure, the reaction temperature must be below 100°C. In comparison, in the current synthesis process, oil acid was employed as the solvent, and the reaction temperature was as high as 215°C. Since graphite and C8 carbon crystals have distinct crystal structures, thus they should have different cohesive energies per atom. Therefore, it is very likely that the C8 quantum dot is the most stable crystal structure under the current specific reaction conditions (solvent, temperature, pressure, and atmosphere), more stable than the graphite quantum dot. Thus the current product is the C8 quantum dots.

In summary, we have synthesized the superdense C8-structured CQDs for the first time and studied their luminescence properties. These CQDs exhibit blue/green double-band luminescence. The combined structural and optical characterizations confirm that the green emission band with a lifetime of tens of nanoseconds originates from the surface C(O)O group of the CQD. The blue emission band with a lifetime of about 6.0ns originates from another surface defect. Our result shows that it is the surface defect center rather than the quantum confinement effect that is responsible for the luminescence of the C8 CQD. The C8 CQD joins the nanoscale carbon family of nanodiamond and graphite QD and may find special applications considering its unique crystal structure.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China Nos. 11074037 and 11274063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.07.056.
